metadata.summary: Confirm requirements represent business need and deliver value using a five-step framework: review quality attributes, choose technique, conduct session, address findings, obtain sign-off.

content.title: Requirements Validation
content.summary: BABOK Task 7.6: confirms that requirements accurately represent the business need and deliver expected value before any downstream commitment. Five steps: review quality attributes, choose validation technique, conduct session, address findings, obtain sign-off. Distinct from Verification (BABOK 7.5), which checks form/ambiguity/testability — Validation asks "are we building the right thing?"

why.p: Requirements are documented but nobody confirms they are correct. When stakeholders sign off without truly understanding, development builds what is written rather than what is meant. The gap between stated and intended requirements is the most expensive defect category — caught at validation it costs a session; caught post-deployment it costs a rework sprint.

list.item: BAKOK Task 7.6 trigger: a requirement or design is ready to be confirmed against business need before downstream commitment.
Before requirements are baselined in a Requirements Repository — validation is the gate.
When a stakeholder need is restated by an agent (summary, paraphrase, transcription) — close the loop on representational drift.
When an existing baselined requirement is challenged by new information (regulation, market signal, data result).
Capstone gate before transitioning from Requirements Analysis to Solution Evaluation.
Pre-elicitation: nothing to validate yet — run elicitation-techniques and requirements-documentation first.
Pure technical-quality concerns (form, style, consistency) — that is Verification, not Validation.
Throwaway prototypes meant to provoke a reaction — the prototype is the elicitation tool, not a sign-off candidate.
Operational/maintenance changes with no new business need (dependency bumps, refactors).
After delivery — use Solution Evaluation (BABOK ch. 8) and feedback-loop measurement instead.

rule.statement: Distinguish validation ("are we building the right thing?") from verification ("are we building it right?"); never run a verification checklist and call it validation.
rule.rationale: Verification checks form, ambiguity, and testability; validation checks business value. Conflating them means value is never confirmed, only syntax is reviewed.

list.item: Validation: question = "does this requirement deliver business value?"; when = before development; focus = requirements accuracy; who = business stakeholders.
Verification: question = "is this requirement well-formed?"; when = after documentation; focus = form/consistency/testability; who = BA, QA, technical reviewers.

rule.statement: Run a verification pass first (form, ambiguity, testability), then run the validation session; do not mix them in a single review.
rule.rationale: When validation sessions debate typos and ambiguities, stakeholders exhaust their time and attention before value questions are reached.

rule.statement: Check all eight quality attributes during verification before any validation session begins.
rule.rationale: Presenting unverified requirements for validation wastes stakeholder time on fixable defects that should have been caught earlier.

list.item: Correct: accurately represents the stakeholder need.
Complete: all necessary information included, no TBDs.
Unambiguous: only one interpretation possible.
Consistent: no conflicts with other requirements.
Testable: can create acceptance tests to verify completion.
Traceable: links to source and purpose (business need, goal, or regulation).
Feasible: can be implemented within project constraints.
Necessary: no gold-plating; removal would create a gap.
Step 1 — Review requirements: check eight quality attributes.
Step 2 — Choose technique: match technique to requirement type (see section below).
Step 3 — Conduct session: walkthrough, stakeholder questions, documented decisions.
Step 4 — Address findings: classify severity (H=blocks design, M=clarification before impl, L=wording), determine root cause, plan resolution.
Step 5 — Obtain sign-off: named approver with authority, documented conditions, outstanding items tracked with owners.

rule.statement: Cap validation sessions at 25 requirements and 90 minutes; run multiple passes rather than one marathon.
rule.rationale: Longer sessions produce rubber-stamp sign-offs as stakeholder attention exhausts; split sessions surface more issues per requirement reviewed.

rule.statement: Select the validation technique by requirement type, not by familiarity; a single technique misses what another would expose.
rule.rationale: Walkthrough alone misses what a prototype review or simulation would expose — BABOK enumerates 11+ validation techniques; each is optimized for a different requirement type.

list.item: Functional requirements: walkthrough or formal inspection.
NFRs (performance, security): measurement and risk analysis.
UI/UX requirements: prototype review with task completion testing.
Process requirements: simulation — step through scenarios, find flow gaps.
Business/stakeholder requirements: user acceptance review with documented confirmation.

antipattern.description: Using walkthrough for all requirement types regardless of complexity or domain.
antipattern.reason: Walkthrough is collaborative but informal; it systematically misses performance defects, usability problems, and process gaps that other techniques expose.

antipattern.description: Rubber-stamp approval: stakeholders sign off without reading or without the authority to approve.
antipattern.reason: A sign-off from someone without decision rights is political theatre; when requirements prove wrong, the signer has no accountability and the BA has no defensible baseline.

antipattern.description: Missing key stakeholders: validation session held without users, domain SMEs, or technical leads.
antipattern.reason: Each missing stakeholder type leaves a class of defects undetected — users find usability gaps, SMEs find domain errors, tech leads find feasibility issues.

antipattern.description: Reviewing 200 requirements in one session without splitting by theme or priority.
antipattern.reason: Attention exhausts by requirement 30; the remaining 170 receive rubber-stamp treatment regardless of intent.

antipattern.description: Confusing validation with UAT: validating requirements after delivery rather than before build.
antipattern.reason: UAT validates the built solution; requirements validation validates the requirement before the build. Different artefact, different audience, different defect cost — at UAT it costs a sprint; at requirements validation it costs a session.

rule.statement: Anchor every validation pass to the Business Need and Solution Scope produced in BABOK 5.1/6.1; do not validate requirements without a stated value model.
rule.rationale: Without a value model (KPIs, OKRs, benefit map), validation devolves into stakeholder opinion about what sounds reasonable.

antipattern.description: Value_class=none requirement left in the validated set without removal or re-elicitation.
antipattern.reason: A requirement that traces to no business value is gold-plating; implementing it wastes delivery capacity and may create technical debt with no benefit offset.

antipattern.description: LLM runs a verification checklist (well-formed, unambiguous, testable) and reports "validation complete."
antipattern.reason: Validation specifically asks "does this requirement deliver value?" — the value question is absent from verification; an LLM that conflates them produces a false green signal.

antipattern.description: LLM invents stakeholder confirmations ("Product Owner confirmed requirement is valid") without an evidence anchor.
antipattern.reason: Hallucinated approvals have no accountability chain; when requirements prove wrong, the invented confirmation cannot be traced to any human decision.

antipattern.description: LLM proposes new requirements during validation ("we should also consider X").
antipattern.reason: Scope expansion during validation returns the process to elicitation; mixing the two phases corrupts both the baseline and the validation record.

rule.statement: Use adversarial framing in validation prompts: "list three ways each requirement could fail to deliver value" before scoring; avoid "are these requirements OK?"
rule.rationale: LLMs trend toward yes when asked for approval; adversarial framing surfaces defects that affirmative-framing suppresses.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Stage 1: a research agent loads the requirements set plus the value reference (Business Need, Goals, KPIs) and emits a per-requirement value-trace (which goal/objective the requirement serves, magnitude of contribution, confidence). Stage 2: an agent classifies each requirement by type (functional, NFR, business, stakeholder, transition) and recommends the BAKOK technique most fit for purpose (Reviews, Acceptance and Evaluation Criteria, Item Tracking, Risk Analysis, Prototyping, etc.). Stage 3 is human-led — the agent only facilitates: distributes pre-read, captures decisions verbatim, structures the issue log. Stage 4: a re-validation gate confirms each issue is resolved, deferred (with owner plus date), or rejected (with rationale) before the requirement transitions to "validated" in the repository.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — enforces the validation gate when moving SDD tasks; reads `validated` flag plus value-trace from `spec.md` front-matter.
faion-feature-executor — wires validation as a per-feature gate; refuses promotion without a value-trace and issue-log resolution.
faion-brainstorm — runs an adversarial diverge pass ("how could this requirement still fail to deliver value?") before the human session; converges to a high-signal questions list.
faion-improver — periodic re-validation sweep: scans baselined requirements in `done/` for value drift against current KPIs.
faion-ba-validator-agent — Sonnet-tier agent producing the value-trace JSON; cheaper than Opus on a structured task.

section.name: prompt-pattern-value-trace
section.code: <role>BA validator (BABOK 7.6). For each requirement, link to the Business Need.</role> <inputs> <business_need>{business_need}</business_need> <goals_kpis>{goals_kpis}</goals_kpis> <requirements>{requirements_set}</requirements> </inputs> <rules> Output JSON. Per requirement: {req_id, goals_supported[], value_class: enabling|direct|indirect|none, contribution: high|medium|low, confidence: high|medium|low, evidence_quote, validation_question} - validation_question: a single yes/no the human reviewer must answer. - If value_class = none: severity = high, recommendation = "remove or re-elicit". - No invented req_ids. evidence_quote must come from inputs, not paraphrase. </rules>

section.name: prompt-pattern-technique-selection
section.code: <task>For each requirement, recommend ONE BABOK validation technique from: [Reviews, Acceptance and Evaluation Criteria, Prototyping, Item Tracking, Risk Analysis and Management, Decision Analysis, Metrics and KPIs]. Output {req_id, technique, rationale, participants_required}. Rationale must cite requirement type (functional/NFR/business/stakeholder/transition).</task>

list.item: Anchor every validation to a stated business value. If you cannot name the goal/KPI/outcome a requirement supports, you are verifying, not validating.
Verify before you validate. Run a verification pass first (form, ambiguity, testability) so the validation session debates value, not typos.
Match technique to requirement type. Functional → walkthrough/inspection; NFR → measurement/risk analysis; UI/UX → prototype review; process → simulation; transition → operational walk.
Cap session size. 25 requirements per session, 90 minutes; multi-pass beats marathon.
Severity has a rule, not a vibe. H = blocks design, M = clarification before implementation, L = wording. Tie to the BABOK Risk Analysis output if available.
Two-pass sign-off. Pass 1 reviews requirements, pass 2 reviews resolved-issue closure. Single-pass approvals quietly leave issues open.
Capture conditions explicitly. "Approved subject to X" without a tracked X = polite rejection. Conditions go on a named owner with status.
Validate iteratively. Per BABOK iterative-development guidance: validate as requirements emerge, not only at baseline.
Persist the validated baseline. Snapshot the validated set (with value-trace) into the SDD `done/` artefact so re-validation has a stable diff target.

antipattern.description: Validation-verification conflation. LLMs default to "is this well-written?" instead of "does this deliver value?"
antipattern.reason: Force the value question: every prompt names the Business Need.

antipattern.description: Hallucinated stakeholders. Models invent SME confirmations.
antipattern.reason: Require an evidence anchor (chat permalink, ticket, signed PDF hash) before any "approved" status.

antipattern.description: Confirmation bias. When asked "are these OK?", LLMs trend toward yes.
antipattern.reason: Use adversarial framing: "list 3 ways each requirement could fail to deliver value" before scoring.

antipattern.description: Severity inflation. Agents tag everything High to look thorough.
antipattern.reason: Anchor severity to the rule above and reject ungrounded H ratings.

antipattern.description: Lost provenance. Agents quote the requirement back to itself instead of the elicitation/business-need source.
antipattern.reason: evidence_quote must come from upstream sources.

antipattern.description: Scope drift. Agents propose new requirements during validation — that returns to elicitation, not validation.
antipattern.reason: Explicitly scope the agent's role: classify existing requirements only, no new proposals.

antipattern.description: Stakeholder simulation. Role-playing "as Product Owner" is a dry run, not a sign-off.
Token cost on large specs. Loading the whole spec per requirement explodes cost.
antipattern.reason: Cache the Business Need / Goals; batch validation by section.

antipattern.description: Locale plus a11y blind spots. Agents pass i18n/a11y requirements silently.
antipattern.reason: Add a per-validation locale plus a11y matrix.

list.item: vale — Prose linter for the Verification half (style, weasel words) so it does not contaminate the validation pass.
markdownlint-cli2 — Catches malformed traceability tables in `spec.md`.
lychee — Verifies cross-document trace links (req → goal → test) resolve.
pandoc — Convert validated requirement set to PDF for stakeholder sign-off (regulated industries).
mermaid-cli — Render value-trace diagram (Goal → Requirement → AC) for the validation packet.
git log -p spec.md — Surface what changed since last validation; informs re-validation scope.
yq / jq — Extract structured front-matter (`validated`, `value_trace`) for gating.
claude (CLI) — Drive the value-trace prompt non-interactively from a pre-commit hook.
Jama Connect (SaaS) — BAKOK-aligned reqs lifecycle, native validation status field, formal sign-off. Heavy for solo.
Polarion (SaaS) — Same audience as Jama; ALM integration. Agents can read/update but UX is human-driven.
IBM DOORS Next (SaaS) — Regulated industries; OSLC links carry value-trace cleanly.
Jira plus Xray (SaaS) — Pragmatic mid-market trace: Requirement issue type → Test → Validation result; agents transition workflow.
Azure DevOps (SaaS) — Work-item state machine encodes "Proposed → Validated → Approved"; reviewers as fields.
Linear (SaaS) — Lightweight; convention-driven validation tags, value-trace in description.
ReqView (Desktop plus cloud) — ReqIF interchange; useful as a neutral export target.
Confluence / Notion (SaaS) — Pre-read packets, draft minutes; weak on trace — pair with a real reqs tool.
Loom / tl;dv / Otter.ai (SaaS) — Recorded validation sessions → transcript → minutes agent.
GitHub Issues (SaaS) — OSS/solo: requirement issue type, validation as a labelled review, sign-off via PR merge.

template.purpose: Requirements review checklist covering general quality and individual requirement checks.
Formal requirements sign-off with scope, conditions, outstanding items, and signature table.
Bash script that fails pre-commit when a requirement in spec.md lacks a value-trace block.
